description: Hallucination, factuality, and grounding problems in LLMs.

facts.context: LLM hallucination refers to model outputs that are:
facts.truth: fluent and confident but factually incorrect or fabricated
distractors: always detectable by the model itself
only occurring in smaller models
eliminated by scaling up model size

facts.context: Closed-book QA (no retrieval) hallucination rates are problematic because:
facts.truth: models confabulate plausible-sounding but false details from parametric memory
distractors: models refuse to answer without retrieval
accuracy exceeds human performance
hallucination only affects open-book settings

facts.context: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) reduces but does not eliminate hallucination because:
facts.truth: models may ignore retrieved context or hallucinate beyond it
distractors: RAG guarantees factual accuracy
retrieval is always perfect
hallucination is impossible with external knowledge

facts.context: LLM-generated citations to academic papers are unreliable because:
facts.truth: fake papers, authors, DOIs are fabricated
distractors: models only cite papers they have been specifically trained on during pretraining
citation accuracy exceeds 95% when verified against databases
fake citations are reliably self-detected by the model's internal verification

facts.context: LLM confidence calibration is problematic because:
facts.truth: models express high confidence even when wrong (overconfidence on errors)
distractors: models are well-calibrated by default
low confidence always indicates errors
calibration improves with model scale alone

facts.context: Teaching LLMs to say 'I don't know' is difficult because:
facts.truth: training incentivizes generating plausible answers over admitting uncertainty
distractors: models naturally refuse uncertain queries
RLHF fully solves this problem
uncertainty is always expressed accurately

facts.context: Entity hallucination (confusing similar entities) occurs because:
facts.truth: models blend attributes of entities with similar names or contexts
distractors: entities are stored in separate memory banks
confusion only happens with fictional entities
larger models never confuse entities

facts.context: Temporal hallucination in LLMs refers to:
facts.truth: mixing up dates, claiming future events happened, or temporal ordering errors
distractors: only affects events after training cutoff
is fully solved by adding timestamps
temporal reasoning is a strength of LLMs

facts.context: Self-consistency decoding (sampling multiple times and voting) helps with:
facts.truth: reducing variance in reasoning but not eliminating systematic hallucinations
distractors: guaranteeing correct answers
eliminating all factual errors
only working for math problems

facts.context: Attribution and source grounding in LLMs is challenging because:
facts.truth: knowledge is distributed across parameters without explicit source tracking
distractors: each fact links to its training source
attribution is solved by attention visualization
models store explicit source metadata

facts.context: Factual recall in LLMs degrades for:
facts.truth: long-tail entities and facts seen rarely during training
distractors: common knowledge more than rare facts
all facts equally regardless of frequency
no correlation with training frequency exists

facts.context: Automated hallucination detection is difficult because:
facts.truth: requires external knowledge or verification that may itself be incomplete
distractors: model logits reliably indicate hallucination
hallucinations have distinctive patterns
detection is a solved problem
